title: About
text: About this site

title: Project Management
text:
NoteBy the end of this section, you should be able toÔÇª




Initialize an R environment using renv for reproducible package management
Organize your project directory with a clear folder structure
Write a README file that documents your project for yourself and others
Use fs::dir_tree() to capture and document your project structure
Understand the purpose of the metadata cleaning and file naming scripts

title: Project Management
text:
Why Project Management?
Good project management is not just about staying organized ÔÇö it is a core part of reproducible research. When you structure your project consistently from the start, you make it easier for collaborators, reviewers, and your future self to understand, rerun, and build on your work.
In this workshop series, we embed reproducibility skills directly into foundational coding instruction. This section introduces two practices you will use in every project going forward: initializing a reproducible R environment and writing documentation.

title: Project Management
text:
Creating an R Environment
We use renv to manage package versions in your project. This ensures that anyone who clones your repository can restore the exact same R environment you used ÔÇö no more ÔÇ£it works on my machineÔÇØ problems.

Navigate to the Console tab (left side of the panel)
Enter getwd() to check that you are in the directory you cloned. If not, navigate there before continuing.

getwd()

Once you are in the correct directory, enter

renv::init()






NoteWhat renv::init() does




Creates an renv/ folder
Creates an renv.lock file
Creates or updates .gitignore to exclude the renv package cache
Snapshots any packages already detected in the project




Restart r







TipIn the console



You can do this with the circular arrow icon in the upper right of the console window.



To capture your current environment, enter:

renv::snapshot()
This records the package versions currently in use.






TipIn the console



If you get a message that says you are ÔÇ£in a project that hasnÔÇÖt been activated yetÔÇØ, choose option 1: Activate the project and use the project library



Take a look at your project directory in the Explorer pane in Positron. In your project directory, you will see new files created by renv:

Ôö£ÔöÇÔöÇ .Rprofile
Ôö£ÔöÇÔöÇ renv.lock
ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ renv/
    ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ .gitignore    # renv's gitignore, scoped only to the renv/ folder
    ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ activate.R
    ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ settings.json
    ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ library/
Now when this repository is cloned by a collaborator or reviewer, renv::restore() can find the renv files you have created and automatically load the matching package versions.

title: Project Management
text:
Write a README file
A README is the entry point to your project. It tells anyone who opens your repository ÔÇö including your future self ÔÇö what the project is, what the data is, and how to use it.

Confirm that you are in the root directory of your workshop folder:

getwd()

In the console, enter:

fs::dir_tree()
This calls the dir_tree function from the fs package - you should see an output that shows you all your project files and their relationships

To write this output to a file, enter this command in the console:

capture.output(fs::dir_tree(), file = "README.txt")

Copy the text in the lines below (stop when you get to step 5) and paste at the top of your README.txt file (open in another window):

This readme file was generated on [YYYY-MM-DD] by [NAME]

GENERAL INFORMATION

Title of Dataset:

Author/Principal Investigator Information

Name: ORCID: Institution: Address: Email:

Date of data collection: provide single date, range, or approximate date; suggested format YYYY-MM-DD)

Geographic location of data collection: provide latitude, longitude, or city/region, State, Country

SHARING/ACCESS INFORMATION

DATA & FILE OVERVIEW

File List:

This README template is a simplified version of Cornell's Readme Template for Data: 
https://ecommons.cornell.edu/entities/publication/6effe964-798e-4c21-aac2-4194709ff230 

Optional: in your README.txt file, write a short description next to these folders:


raw_data (original data goes here)
processed data (clean data goes here)
scripts (code to for analyses goes here)
outputs (analyses outputs go here)

title: Project Management
text:
Scripts for Project Management
The following scripts are used in this workshop series. They are designed to be run in your local Positron session ÔÇö copy them into your scripts/ folder and run them from there.






Important



Before Running These Scripts
These scripts load data files from your local raw_data/ folder. Make sure you have completed Exercises 1 and 2 and that your folder structure is in place before running them.



Metadata Cleaning for Analysis
This script creates the metadata file needed for further analysis, using the same dataset contrasted with Excel earlier in this workshop.
# Metadata Cleaning for Analysis
# This script creates the metadata file that we need for further analysis

library(dplyr)
library(stringr)

# Load data
metadata <- read.csv("raw_data/GSE113754_filtered_metadata.csv",
                     header = TRUE,
                     stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

# Create the desired output format
metadata_parsed <- metadata %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Clean up genotype column - convert "Shank3 Mutant" to "Shank3"
    genotype = case_when(
      str_detect(genotype, "WT") ~ "WT",
      str_detect(genotype, "Shank3") ~ "Shank3",
      TRUE ~ as.character(genotype)
    ),

    # Extract condition from characteristics column,
    # format and store in a new column called "Condition"
    Condition = case_when(
      str_detect(characteristics, "homecage control") ~ "_normal",
      str_detect(characteristics, "sleep deprivation") ~ "_sleep_deprived",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    ),

    # Create Geno_Cond column by combining results from the
    # "genotype" and "Condition" column mutations
    Geno_Cond = paste(genotype, str_remove(Condition, "^_"), sep = "_")
  ) %>%
  # Select only the columns you want in the final output
  dplyr::select(Sample = 1, genotype, Condition, Geno_Cond)

# Check the result
head(metadata_parsed)

# Save to CSV
write.csv(metadata_parsed, "Shank3_metadata_parsed.csv", row.names = FALSE)


Automate File Naming
This script demonstrates how to automatically add a timestamp to any R function and store the result in your outputs/ folder as a new file each time you call it. This ensures you never accidentally overwrite a previous result.
# Automate File Naming
# Automatically timestamps output files and stores them in the outputs/ folder

library(readxl)
library(writexl)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

# Configuration section ÔÇö update these paths for your project
CONFIG <- list(
  input_file = "raw_data/GSE113754_GeneLevel_Raw_data.xlsx",
  output_folder = "outputs",
  prefix = "LD_ProjectARCS"
)

# Function to generate timestamped filename
generate_filename <- function(prefix, analysis_type = "", extension = ".xlsx") {
  timestamp <- format(Sys.time(), "%Y%m%dT%H%M%S")  # ISO 8601 format
  if (analysis_type != "") {
    filename <- paste0(timestamp, "_", prefix, "_", analysis_type, extension)
  } else {
    filename <- paste0(timestamp, "_", prefix, extension)
  }
  return(filename)
}

# Function to ensure output directory exists
ensure_output_dir <- function(output_folder) {
  if (!dir.exists(output_folder)) {
    dir.create(output_folder, recursive = TRUE)
    cat("Created output directory:", output_folder, "\n")
  }
}

# Function to load Excel data
load_excel_data <- function(file_path, sheet = 1) {
  cat("Loading data from:", file_path, "\n")
  data <- read_excel(file_path, sheet = sheet)
  cat("Data loaded successfully. Dimensions:", dim(data), "\n")
  return(data)
}

# Function to save results
save_results <- function(data, output_folder, prefix, analysis_type = "") {
  ensure_output_dir(output_folder)
  filename <- generate_filename(prefix, analysis_type)
  full_path <- file.path(output_folder, filename)
  write_xlsx(data, full_path)
  cat("Results saved to:", full_path, "\n")
  return(full_path)
}

# Analysis 1: Basic summary statistics
analysis_summary_stats <- function(df, output_folder, prefix) {
  cat("\n=== Running Summary Statistics Analysis ===\n")
  summary_data <- df %>%
    summarise_if(is.numeric, list(
      mean = ~mean(., na.rm = TRUE),
      median = ~median(., na.rm = TRUE),
      sd = ~sd(., na.rm = TRUE),
      min = ~min(., na.rm = TRUE),
      max = ~max(., na.rm = TRUE)
    ))
  save_path <- save_results(summary_data, output_folder, prefix, "summary_stats")
  return(summary_data)
}

# Analysis 2: Filtered data analysis
analysis_filtered_data <- function(df, output_folder, prefix,
                                   filter_condition = NULL) {
  cat("\n=== Running Filtered Data Analysis ===\n")
  if (!is.null(filter_condition)) {
    processed_data <- df %>%
      filter(eval(parse(text = filter_condition))) %>%
      arrange(desc(if(is.numeric(.[,1])) .[,1] else row_number()))
  } else {
    processed_data <- df %>%
      arrange(if(is.numeric(.[,1])) desc(.[,1]) else .[,1])
  }
  save_path <- save_results(processed_data, output_folder, prefix,
                            "filtered_data")
  return(processed_data)
}

# Analysis 3: Grouped analysis
analysis_grouped <- function(df, output_folder, prefix,
                             group_by_col = NULL) {
  cat("\n=== Running Grouped Analysis ===\n")
  if (!is.null(group_by_col) && group_by_col %in% names(df)) {
    grouped_data <- df %>%
      group_by(across(all_of(group_by_col))) %>%
      summarise_if(is.numeric, list(
        count = ~n(),
        mean = ~mean(., na.rm = TRUE),
        sum = ~sum(., na.rm = TRUE)
      ), .groups = 'drop')
  } else {
    first_char_col <- names(select_if(df, is.character))[1]
    if (!is.null(first_char_col)) {
      grouped_data <- df %>%
        count(across(all_of(first_char_col)), name = "frequency") %>%
        arrange(desc(frequency))
    } else {
      grouped_data <- data.frame(note = "No grouping column available")
    }
  }
  save_path <- save_results(grouped_data, output_folder, prefix,
                            "grouped_analysis")
  return(grouped_data)
}

# Main execution function
run_analysis <- function() {
  cat("=== Starting Analysis Pipeline ===\n")
  cat("Timestamp:", format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"), "\n\n")

  original_data <- load_excel_data(CONFIG$input_file)
  working_data <- original_data

  results <- list()
  results$summary <- analysis_summary_stats(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)
  results$filtered <- analysis_filtered_data(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)
  results$grouped <- analysis_grouped(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)

  cat("\n=== Analysis Pipeline Complete ===\n")
  cat("All results saved to:", CONFIG$output_folder, "\n")
  return(results)
}

# Helper function to run individual analyses
run_single_analysis <- function(analysis_type, ...) {
  original_data <- load_excel_data(CONFIG$input_file)
  working_data <- original_data
  switch(analysis_type,
    "summary"  = analysis_summary_stats(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix),
    "filtered" = analysis_filtered_data(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix, ...),
    "grouped"  = analysis_grouped(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix, ...),
    stop("Unknown analysis type. Use 'summary', 'filtered', or 'grouped'")
  )
}

# To use this script:
# 1. Update the CONFIG section above with your file paths
# 2. Run all analyses:
results <- run_analysis()

# Or run individual analyses:
summary_result <- run_single_analysis("summary")
# filtered_result <- run_single_analysis("filtered",
#                      filter_condition = "column_name > 50")
grouped_result <- run_single_analysis("grouped",
                    group_by_col = "category")






Note



Can YouÔÇª?
Ô£ô Initialize an R environment with renv::init()?
Ô£ô Explain what renv.lock is and why it matters for reproducibility?
Ô£ô Use fs::dir_tree() to inspect your project structure?
Ô£ô Write and populate a README file for a project?
Ô£ô Explain what the metadata cleaning script does at a high level?
Ô£ô Explain why timestamped file naming helps prevent data loss?

title: Reports
text:
NoteBy the end of this section, you should be able toÔÇª




Convert a plain text file into a Quarto document (.qmd)
Render a .qmd file to HTML and understand what ÔÇ£localhostÔÇØ means
Embed R code chunks into a Quarto document with correct syntax
Use fs::dir_tree() to automatically generate and annotate a project structure in your README
Re-render a document to reflect updated content
Create a Quarto document using a journal template (PLOS)
Run a gene variability analysis and interpret summary statistics
Generate and save a publication-quality expression heatmap

title: Reports
text:
Why Reports?
Quarto documents let you combine code, results, and narrative text in a single file that can be rendered to HTML, PDF, or Word. This means your analysis and its documentation live together ÔÇö when your code changes, your report updates automatically. This is a core reproducibility practice.

title: Reports
section: Convert README.txt to a Quarto Document
text:
Convert README.txt to a Quarto Document

Navigate to your README.txt file in your ARCS project folder (PC: Windows Explorer; Mac: Finder)
Right click on the file and select Rename
Change the file extension from .txt to .qmd (Mac: type .qmd after README)
Accept the file extension change and save the file
Go back to Positron and open your README.qmd file
Add triple backticks ( ``` ) before and after your READMEÔÇÖs file structure tree to format it as a code block
In Positron, click the Render button to render your document
A browser window should open and display your README.qmd file as HTML







NoteWhat is localhost?



Notice that the browser windowÔÇÖs address starts with localhost followed by a port number. This means your computer is serving your .qmd file locally to itself ÔÇö the file is not on the internet, it is being previewed from your own machine.

title: Reports
text:
Automate README.qmd Updates
Instead of manually updating your README every time your project structure changes, we can write an R code chunk that generates the file tree automatically each time you render.

Check whether you have the fs package installed:

library(fs)
If you get an error, install it first:
install.packages("fs")

Copy the script below:

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| results: asis

library(fs)

# Get the actual structure
tree_output <- capture.output(dir_tree(".", recurse = 2))

# Define comments for specific files/folders
comments <- list(
  "raw_data"       = " (original data goes here)",
  "processed_data" = " (clean data goes here)",
  "scripts"        = " (code goes here)",
  "outputs"        = " (results and charts go here)",
  "README.qmd"     = " (project documentation)"
)

# Add comments to matching lines
for(item in seq_along(tree_output)) {
  line <- tree_output[item]
  for(name in names(comments)) {
    if(grepl(name, line)) {
      tree_output[item] <- paste0(line, comments[[name]])
      break
    }
  }
}

# Print the result
cat("```\n")
cat(paste(tree_output, collapse = "\n"))
cat("\n```\n")






NoteWhat this script does




dir_tree() generates a visual tree of your project folder up to 2 levels deep
capture.output() stores that tree as a character vector
The comments list defines annotations to add next to specific folders or files
The for loop finds matching lines and appends the annotations
cat() prints the result as a formatted code block in your rendered document




Paste this script into your README.qmd file in between the triple backticks where your file tree currently appears
Next to the opening triple backticks, add {r} to make it an R code chunk ÔÇö it should look like this: ```{r}
Save your README.qmd file
Click the Render button in Positron
Go back to your browser window to see the result
Make any changes you would like to the comments <- list() section and re-render to see them reflected

title: Reports
section: Create a Quarto Document for Publishing
text:
Create a Quarto Document for Publishing
In this exercise you will create a publication-quality document using a PLOS journal template, run a gene variability analysis, and generate a heatmap figure.

Setup

Check whether you have the following packages installed:

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(scales)
If any are missing, install them in your console:
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("readxl")
install.packages("scales")






TipChecking installed packages in Positron



You can also check in the Extensions pane by searching for the package name.




Install the PLOS Template

In your Terminal tab, run the following commands one at a time:

quarto add quarto-journals/plos
quarto use template quarto-journals/plos






NoteWhat this does



This installs a Quarto extension that formats your document to match PLOS journal submission requirements ÔÇö including layout, fonts, and figure formatting. This is the same approach used for many other journal templates available through quarto-journals.




Gene Variability Analysis

Copy the code below and paste it into your publication.qmd file underneath the heading ## Gene Variability Analysis. Start your code block with ```{r} and close it with ```.







NoteAbout the #| lines



These are chunk options ÔÇö they tell Quarto how to handle the output when this code runs inside a .qmd file. message: false suppresses package loading messages, and warning: false suppresses warnings.


#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Prepare data - rename the first column and use both ID columns
gene_data_long <- gene_data %>%
  rename(ensembl_id = `...1`) %>%
  filter(!is.na(gene_symbol), gene_symbol != "") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(ensembl_id, gene_symbol),
               names_to = "sample",
               values_to = "expression")

# Calculate variance for each gene across all samples
gene_variability <- gene_data_long %>%
  group_by(gene_symbol, ensembl_id) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_expression        = mean(expression, na.rm = TRUE),
    variance               = var(expression, na.rm = TRUE),
    coefficient_of_variation = sd(expression, na.rm = TRUE) / mean_expression,
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

# Identify top 15 most variable genes
top_genes <- gene_variability %>%
  arrange(desc(variance)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15)

# Display summary statistics
cat("Total genes analyzed:", nrow(gene_variability), "\n")
cat("Mean variance across all genes:",
    round(mean(gene_variability$variance, na.rm = TRUE), 2), "\n")
cat("Most variable gene:", top_genes$gene_symbol[1],
    "with variance =", round(top_genes$variance[1], 2), "\n")

# Show the top 10 most variable genes
cat("\nTop 10 most variable genes:\n")
print(top_genes %>%
        select(gene_symbol, variance) %>%
        slice_head(n = 10) %>%
        mutate(variance = round(variance, 2)))


Data Visualization

Copy the code below and paste it into your publication.qmd file underneath the heading ## Data Visualization. Start your code block with ```{r} and close it with ```.

#| fig-cap: "Expression heatmap showing scaled expression levels of the
#|   15 most variable genes across all samples. Blue indicates
#|   below-average expression, white indicates average expression,
#|   and red indicates above-average expression for each gene."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

# Prepare heatmap data
heatmap_data <- gene_data_long %>%
  filter(gene_symbol %in% top_genes$gene_symbol) %>%
  left_join(top_genes %>% select(gene_symbol, variance),
            by = "gene_symbol") %>%
  group_by(gene_symbol) %>%
  mutate(scaled_expr = scale(expression)[, 1]) %>%
  ungroup()

# Create heatmap visualization
expression_heatmap <- heatmap_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sample,
             y = reorder(gene_symbol, variance),
             fill = scaled_expr)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low   = "blue",
    mid   = "white",
    high  = "red",
    name  = "Scaled\nExpression",
    limits = c(-3, 3),
    oob   = scales::squish
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Expression Variability Heatmap: Top 15 Most Variable Genes",
    subtitle = "Genes ordered by variance (highest at top)",
    x        = "Sample ID",
    y        = "Gene Symbol"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x  = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 10, face = "italic"),
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid   = element_blank()
  )

print(expression_heatmap)

# Save high-resolution figure
ggsave(paste0("outputs/", timestamp, "_expression_heatmap.png"),
       plot   = expression_heatmap,
       width  = 10,
       height = 6,
       dpi    = 300)

Render your publication.qmd document to see your PLOS-formatted article







TipSaving your figure



Notice the ggsave() call at the end of the visualization block. This saves a high-resolution .png to your outputs/ folder with a timestamp in the filename ÔÇö the same automated file naming pattern introduced in the Project Management section.









NoteCan YouÔÇª?




Ô£ô Convert a .txt file to a .qmd file and render it to HTML?
Ô£ô Explain what localhost means when previewing a rendered document?
Ô£ô Add an R code chunk to a .qmd file with correct syntax?
Ô£ô Use dir_tree() and capture.output() to auto-generate a project tree in your README?
Ô£ô Modify the comments list and re-render to see updated annotations?
Ô£ô Install and apply a Quarto journal template from the terminal?
Ô£ô Explain what the gene variability analysis is doing at a high level?
Ô£ô Render a publication-quality heatmap and save it to your outputs/ folder?

title: Data Structures in R
text:
NoteBy the end of this section, you should be able toÔÇª




Load a package and import .csv files into R using read.csv()
Inspect the structure, dimensions, and data types of an object
Identify and locate missing values using is.na() and which()
Use indexing to retrieve and modify specific elements in a data frame
Explore unique values and frequency counts using unique() and table()
Clean and subset a data frame by renaming columns, removing columns, and recoding values
Harmonize two data frames so they are compatible for downstream analysis
Save cleaned data frames to .csv files using write.csv()

title: Data Structures in R
text:
Opening Your Project
Before importing any data, make sure you are working inside your R project and that your working directory is set correctly.
getwd()

title: Data Structures in R
text:
Importing Data
We use the readr package to import data. To learn more about any package, run help(package = "package_name") in your Positron console.
library(readr)
We will be working with two tables from the GREIN dataset. Import them using read.csv(). To learn more about any function, run ?function_name in your Positron console.
1. Raw counts at the gene level:
rawc <- read.csv("raw_data/GSE113754_GeneLevel_Raw_data_3.csv")
2. Sample metadata ÔÇö information about the sequenced samples:
info <- read.csv("raw_data/GSE113754_filtered_metadata.csv")

title: Data Structures in R
text:
Inspecting Data Structures

Class and Type
Find what type of structure an object has with class():

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Find the type of data stored in an object with typeof():

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






TipExercise 1



Find the type of data stored in a specific column of an object. Use $ after the object name to access a column:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.




Dimensions and Structure
Find the dimensions of both tables using dim():

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Get more detailed information about the overall structure of an object with str():

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Summary Statistics
To get an idea of the type of data and some minimal statistics, use summary():

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






NoteWhat summary() returns



For numeric vectors:



Output
Description




Min.
The minimum value


1st Qu.
The first quartile (25th percentile)


Median
The median (50th percentile)


Mean
The arithmetic mean


3rd Qu.
The third quartile (75th percentile)


Max.
The maximum value



For character variables:



Output
Description




Length
Total number of elements


Class
Data type ÔÇö will show "character"


Mode
Storage mode ÔÇö will show "character"

title: Data Structures in R
text:
Identifying Missing Values
To check whether an object contains any NA or missing values, we can use several functions.
For a quick check of whether any missing values exist:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
For a more granular analysis, use is.na(). Try it on a simple example first:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Note



This function can be impractical when looking at a large data frame like rawc ÔÇö it returns a value for every single element. For large objects, anyNA() or which(is.na()) are more useful.

title: Data Structures in R
text:
Manipulating Specific Data Points
The selection and inspection of specific elements or subsets of data are made easier in R by the use of an elementÔÇÖs numerical position, or index.






ImportantIndexing in R



In R, element numbering starts at 1. Note: Python uses 0-based indexing.
Vectors ÔÇö access by position:
vector[1]       # first element
Matrices and Data Frames ÔÇö use a comma to separate row and column:
table[1, 2]     # element in row 1, column 2
table[1059, ]   # entire row 1059
table[, 3]      # entire column 3


We know that rawc has an NA value in a specific column ($GSM3119015). To find the exact row where it occurs, we can nest is.na() inside which():

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






NoteConditional Operators in R






Operator
Meaning




==
equal


!=
not equal


>
greater than


<
less than


>=
greater than or equal to


<=
less than or equal to


%in%
included in





We can confirm the NA value is in row 1059 of rawc by inspecting that row:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






TipExercise 2



Fix the NA value by using its positional coordinates ÔÇö row and column index or name:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

text:
Exploring Unique Values
LetÔÇÖs turn to the info object and inspect what unique values exist using unique() and table():

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
For an organized view across the whole table, apply the same functions with sapply():

title: Data Structures in R
text:
Data Cleaning and Subsetting

Renaming Columns
Change specific column names using colnames() and []:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Note



colnames() retrieves a vector, so we only need [] without a comma ÔÇö a vector is a one-dimensional structure and does not need row/column indexing.




Removing a Column
Remove the redundant genotype.ch1 column using its index number:

Recoding Values
Clean up the genotype column ÔÇö convert "Shank3 Mutant" to "Shank3":

title: Data Structures in R
text:
Data Harmonization
Data harmonization is the process of collecting and unifying data from various sources and formats into a consistent, standardized format ÔÇö making it compatible for integration and analysis.
We have two tables that need to be harmonized before conducting downstream analysis such as differential gene expression analysis. Specifically, the samples in rawc (raw counts) must be in the same number and order as in info_clean (metadata).
Check whether the column names of rawc match the sample IDs in info_clean:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
LetÔÇÖs take a closer look at each:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
To harmonize the two objects:
Step 1: Create a gene_id vector by merging gene ID and gene symbol columns using paste(), separating them with |:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Step 2: Create a new data frame keeping only the columns that match info_clean$sample_id, and set gene_id as row names so it is not interpreted as data:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Save Cleaned Tables
Save the cleaned metadata and raw counts tables as .csv files in your processed_data/ folder:
write.csv(info_clean, "processed_data/Shank3_metadata_clean.csv")
write.csv(rawc_clean, "processed_data/Shank3_rawCounts_clean.csv")






Note



write.csv() is kept as a static block ÔÇö it writes to your local file system and cannot be run in the browser.









NoteCan YouÔÇª?




Ô£ô Load a package with library() and import a .csv with read.csv()?
Ô£ô Use class(), typeof(), dim(), and str() to inspect an object?
Ô£ô Explain what summary() returns for numeric vs.┬ácharacter variables?
Ô£ô Use is.na() and which() to locate a missing value?
Ô£ô Retrieve and modify a specific element using row and column indexing?
Ô£ô Use unique() and table() to explore values in a column?
Ô£ô Rename and remove columns in a data frame?
Ô£ô Use ifelse() and grepl() to recode values in a column?
Ô£ô Explain what data harmonization is and why it matters?
Ô£ô Save a cleaned data frame to a .csv file with write.csv()?

title: Fundamentals of R
text:
NoteBy the end of this section, you should be able toÔÇª




Find and set your working directory in R
Write and run basic arithmetic and code in R
Create and use R objects to store information
Build vectors and understand the main data types in R
Understand the difference between data frames and matrices
Use functions like c(), paste0(), data.frame(), and cbind()

title: Fundamentals of R
text:
Setting Up the Working Directory
What is your working directory? Run getwd() to find out.






TipLearning About Any Function



To learn more about any function, run ?function_name in the console. For example: ?getwd


?getwd()
For the sake of simplifying reading data into R, we need to set our working directory to the folder where our data is located. We will be working from a folder called ARCS_project ÔÇö this is where your data files and script files live for this workshop series.
In Positron you can set your working directory using the Files pane:

Navigate to your ARCS_project folder
Click the gear icon in the Files pane toolbar
Select ÔÇ£Set as Working DirectoryÔÇØ

Notice in your console that a line of code will appear similar to this:
# Mac example
setwd("~/Desktop/ARCS_project")

# Windows example
setwd("C:/Users/YourName/Desktop/ARCS_project")
setwd() is the programmatic way to establish a working directory ÔÇö you just need to write the full path to your ARCS_project folder on your machine.






TipVerify Your Working Directory



After setting it, confirm youÔÇÖre in the right place:
getwd()
You should see the path ending in ARCS_project.

title: Fundamentals of R
section: Four Basic Rules for Writing Code
text:
Four Basic Rules for Writing Code

Use # to annotate and document your code
Use a noticeable pattern to break between coding chunks

#----- Section Title ------#
########################

Numbers and basic arithmetic work just as on paper

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






Note



Using the colon : gives you a range of numbers between two values:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Letters must be written between quotation marks ""







NoteException



Characters defining the name of an object can be written without quotation marks.








TipExercise 1




Write and run: Hello world!
Now write and run: "Hello world!"


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
What is different about the second stepÔÇÖs output?

title: Fundamentals of R
text:
Storing Information in R Objects
Objects simplify data manipulation. Objects result from assigning to a name values or data we want to transform/analyse. This data can then be easily used and re-used. This way, we donÔÇÖt have to repeat operations.
To create an object, we need:
name <- data
This way we are assigning to objects on the left the values on the right.






TipExercise 2



Run the following lines one at a time and observe what happens:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
You have now stored information in the object x. That object is now visible in the Variables pane in Positron.
When you run x, R retrieves the information stored in it.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
You can manipulate the information stored in the object by simply using the object name, saving you from repeating operations.
LetÔÇÖs play a little more! Fill in the blanks:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Fundamentals of R
section: Tricks and Rules for Creating R Objects
text:
Tricks and Rules for Creating R Objects

It is good practice to always use <- instead of =







TipKeyboard Shortcut for <-



PC: Alt + -
Mac: Option + - 



An R object can have almost any name. However, there are some important considerations:







ImportantNaming Rules



Ô£ù No empty spaces ÔÇö the code will not run
Ô£ù Cannot start with numbers (2x is not valid, but x2 is)
Ô£ù Cannot have the same name as functions ÔÇö it gets confusing 








TipExercise 3




Assign your age to an object with a descriptive name:


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Subtract your object from the current year to obtain your birth year:


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Fundamentals of R
text:
Vectors
A vector is the most common and basic data structure in R. A vector is composed by a series of values of one specific data type.
There are 5 main types of data:



Type
Description
Example




double / numeric
Numbers with decimals
1.2


integer
Whole numbers
10L


complex
Numbers with imaginary component
2i


logical
True or False
TRUE


character
String of characters
"Sofia"



To create a vector we use the function c().






NoteFunctions, Arguments, and Packages



Functions like c() are ÔÇ£canned scriptsÔÇØ that automate more complicated sets of commands including operations and assignments.

A function often (but not always) takes one or more inputs called arguments
A function often (but not always) returns a value
Many functions are predefined, or can be made available by importing R packages (more on that later)

To learn more enter ?c() in your computerÔÇÖs terminal.








TipExercise 4




Make a vector by combining the ages of four individuals you know with the function c(), separating the ages with commas, and assign that vector to an object.


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Now we can manipulate the information in the vector by simply using the name of the object ÔÇö no need for quotation marks "".

Subtract the vector you created in step 1. from the current year:


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Assign the result of step 2. to a new object


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Fundamentals of R
text:
Tabular Data
Matrices and data frames are both two-dimensional data structures used to store tabular data (rows and columns), but they differ primarily in the types of data they can contain and their underlying structure.









Data Frame
Matrix




Data types
Can mix types (heterogeneous)
Single type only (homogeneous)


Common use
Data processing, statistics, plotting
Mathematical operations


Structure
List of equal-length vectors
Vector with dimension attributes



Data frames are what we will most commonly use for data processing, statistics, and plotting of tabular data in R.






WarningBefore ContinuingÔÇª



Make sure you have run the code blocks in the Vectors section above. The examples below depend on the objects FamAges and FamYear that you created there.


LetÔÇÖs pack the two vectors we have created into a data frame using data.frame():

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






NoteInspecting Large Tables



Use head() or tail() to inspect tables with more than 6 rows. Learn more by running ?head and ?tail in your Positron console.


The advantage of using data frame format instead of matrix is that we can combine different data types in the same table.






TipExercise 5




Assign to a new object a vector containing the names of your family members with the correspondent ages described in the vector we previously created:


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Add the vector containing the names to the data frame we created using cbind(). (Learn more with ?cbind in your Positron console)


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

What do you think will happen if you use cbind() to add a character vector to your matrix? Run it and see ÔÇö was your prediction correct?


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Tidyverse
text:
NoteBy the end of this section, you should be able toÔÇª




Use pacman::p_load() to install and load multiple packages in a reproducible way
Use the here package to build file paths relative to your project root
Explain the difference between read_csv() and read.csv(), and what a tibble is
Inspect a data frame using view(), head(), and str()
Use the pipe operator %>% to chain multiple data cleaning steps together
Clean column names automatically using clean_names()
Rename, recode, and create new columns using rename(), mutate(), and case_when()
Combine two columns into a new variable using paste0()
Select and reorder columns using select() and everything()
Handle missing values using replace_na()
Harmonize two data frames so their columns and row identifiers align
Save cleaned data frames to .csv files using write_csv() and write.csv()

title: Tidyverse
text:
Load Packages
The pacman package helps ensure your script is reproducible ÔÇö it automatically installs and loads the packages needed to run your code, even on someone elseÔÇÖs computer. You can add to the list as more packages are needed.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Tidyverse
section: Importing Data with here() and readr
text:
Importing Data with here() and readr
The here package improves reproducibility and sharing by creating file paths relative to your R projectÔÇÖs root directory, eliminating the need for hard-coded absolute paths ÔÇö as long as your script file is saved in the same folder as your R project.

Confirm Your Working Directory
Run this to make sure you are currently in the correct R project:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






NoteIf you are not in the correct project



You can set the project root manually ÔÇö replace the path with your actual folder location:
# set_here("path/to/your/desired/folder")




Import the Data Files
# Import the raw counts data
raw_data <- read_csv(here("raw_data", "GSE113754_GeneLevel_Raw_data_3.csv"))

# Import the metadata
raw_metadata <- read_csv(here("raw_data", "GSE113754_filtered_metadata.csv"))






Noteread_csv() vs read.csv()



This section uses read_csv() from the Tidyverse rather than read.csv() from base R. Key differences:



Feature
read_csv()
read.csv()




Column types
Explicit (col_double, etc.)
Inferred


Speed
Faster on large files
Slower


Output
Tibble
Data frame


Column names
Preserved as-is
May be altered


Row labels
Not used
Supported



A tibble is a modern data frame that prints more cleanly, displays better on screen, and produces more informative output from functions like str().

title: Tidyverse
text:
Cleaning the Metadata

Inspect the Data
Physically look at the data you just imported:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Use head() to see the first 6 rows, column names, and data types:

TipEXTRA: Count unique values in a column



We can find the unique values within a variable and their counts using count() ÔÇö similar to the unique() and table() exercises from the Data Structures section:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.




What Changes Do We Need to Make?

Fix column names
Rename characteristic to condition
Clean up values under condition
Clean up values under genotype
Combine condition and genotype into a new column geno_cond
Keep columns in this order: sample_id, genotype, condition, geno_cond
Make values under sample_id lowercase







TipWorking with the pipe %>%



Each step below builds on the previous one using the pipe operator %>%. You can run the code up to any %>% to inspect the result at that point ÔÇö this is a great way to debug and understand how the data changes at each step.




Step 1: Fix All Column Names
clean_names() standardizes all column names to lowercase with underscores:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Step 2: Rename Columns
Rename column x1 to sample_id and characteristics to condition:

Step 3: Recode Values in condition

If the value includes "homecage" ÔåÆ change to "normal"
If the value includes "sleep deprivation" ÔåÆ change to "sleep_deprived"


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






NoteBase R inside the Tidyverse



Notice that grepl() is a base R function ÔÇö you can use base R freely within Tidyverse pipelines. This is the same function used in the Data Structures section with ifelse().




Step 4: Recode Values in genotype

If the value includes "WT" ÔåÆ change to "WT"
If the value includes "shank3" ÔåÆ change to "shank3"


Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Step 5: Create geno_cond Column
Combine genotype and condition into a new variable:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Step 6: Select and Reorder Columns
Keep only the columns we need in the correct order:

Step 7: Lowercase sample_id
Make sample_id values lowercase ÔÇö this will be important for harmonizing with the raw counts data in the next section:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Tidyverse
text:
Cleaning the Raw Counts Data

Inspect the Data

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

What Issues Did You Find?

Column 1 has no label and contains row numbers inserted in error
Column 2 has no label
Column 3 (gene symbol) has a space in its name
All columns beginning with GSM are uppercase







TipEXTRA: Search for NA values



Use filter(), if_any(), and everything() with is.na() to search all rows and columns for missing values:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The NA value identified here will be addressed in the cleaning steps below.




Cleaning Steps

clean_names()
Gives columns 1 and 2 auto-generated labels, replaces spaces in column names with underscores, and converts all uppercase letters to lowercase:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


rename()
Change the auto-generated name for column 2 (x2) to gene_id:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


mutate() ÔÇö Handle the NA Value
Replace the NA value under gsm3119015 with 0:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






ImportantA note on handling missing values



Replacing NA with 0 is used here for demonstration purposes. In practice, the right approach depends on your data and analysis ÔÇö options include removing rows, leaving the NA, or calculating an imputed value. Always think carefully before replacing missing data.




mutate() ÔÇö Create a Combined Gene ID Column
Create a new variable that combines gene_id and gene_symbol separated by |. Notice that paste() from base R works seamlessly inside a Tidyverse pipeline:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


select()
Make gene_id_symbol the first column, keep everything else in the same order using everything(), and remove x1, gene_symbol, and gene_id using the - operator:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Tidyverse
section: EXTRA: Harmonizing the Two Data Frames
text:
EXTRA: Harmonizing the Two Data Frames
These extra steps align the structure of clean_data with clean_meta_data so they are ready for downstream analysis such as differential gene expression.

Extra 1: Set Row IDs
Use column_to_rownames() from the tibble package to convert the first column from a data variable into row identifiers:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Extra 2: Reorder Columns to Match Metadata
Reorder the columns in clean_data to match the order of sample_id values in clean_meta_data:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.


Extra 3: Verify Harmonization
Run a quick check to confirm that the column names in clean_data and the sample_id values in clean_meta_data match exactly:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Tidyverse
text:
Save Cleaned Data
Save clean_data and clean_meta_data locally using here():
# Saves with row labels (use read.csv to reload with row labels intact)
write.csv(clean_data, here("processed_data", "clean_data_label.csv"))

# Saves without row labels (standard clean CSV)
write_csv(clean_data, here("processed_data", "clean_data.csv"))

# Save clean metadata
write_csv(clean_meta_data, here("processed_data", "clean_meta_data.csv"))






Notewrite_csv() vs write.csv()






Function
Row labels
Package




write_csv()
Not included
Tidyverse (readr)


write.csv()
Included
Base R



Use write.csv() when you need to preserve row labels (as in clean_data_label.csv). Use write_csv() for standard clean output.
These blocks are static and cannot be run in the browser ÔÇö they write to your local file system and must be run in your Positron session.









NoteCan YouÔÇª?

Ô£ô Use clean_names() to standardize column names automatically?
Ô£ô Use rename(), mutate(), and case_when() to recode values?
Ô£ô Use select() and everything() to reorder and drop columns?
Ô£ô Replace NA values using replace_na()?
Ô£ô Explain what data harmonization means and verify it with setequal()?
